workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: The collected PM sources show a mid-level consumer Product Manager working mostly through digital artifacts, analysis, written communication, and stakeholder conversation. The real work is not physical manipulation; it is reading messy dashboards and feedback, framing the product problem, choosing focused bets, writing decision artifacts, and aligning design, engineering, data, support, and leadership around tradeoffs.

sourceEvidence: The PM sources describe meetings, documents, dashboards, roadmaps, and conversations rather than physical workplace manipulation.
The social trip-planning context is a consumer software product, so concrete work surfaces are digital artifacts.
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: No physical playground is needed; realistic action should happen through dashboard, document, Slack, and voice-meeting scenes.

sourceEvidence: PM day-in-the-life transcripts emphasize reviewing docs, roadmaps, analytics, design work, and cross-functional planning artifacts.
Reddit PM discussions repeatedly describe requirements, roadmaps, customer feedback, prioritization, and async updates.
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Include multiple desk-work scenes with visible source artifacts and realistic app windows.

sourceEvidence: PM practitioners frame the role around deciding what problem matters, what to build or test, and what tradeoffs to accept.
Startup and UX sources emphasize validation and decision quality before committing engineering effort.
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: The core graded tasks should require synthesis, prioritization, hypothesis framing, and tradeoff reasoning.

sourceEvidence: PM work commonly produces PRDs, decision memos, roadmap notes, launch updates, and stakeholder summaries.
Sources describe PM communication as making decisions portable across teams.
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Require the student to produce a problem brief, research prep, PRD slice, and stakeholder Slack response.

sourceEvidence: PM sources describe customer conversations and stakeholder alignment as common parts of the job.
Influence without authority appears repeatedly in practitioner discussion.
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: Include a remote voice meeting with a user and async alignment with engineering/design.

sourceEvidence: Day-in-the-life sources describe context switching across meetings, Slack, docs, and quick decisions.
Reddit PM sources mention calendar fragmentation and ambiguity.
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Use briefing context to show time pressure, but grade active artifacts rather than waiting.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: The active scene mix mirrors a PM day: source review, current-flow audit, structured evidence synthesis, research-question preparation, a voice user interview, and a PRD/decision memo. The player works from visible product artifacts and produces role-specific PM deliverables.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: The simulator keeps physical work rare and makes the concrete action happen in digital work surfaces: Notion-style notes, a remote user call, a document editor, and Slack.

intro.title: Welcome to Roamly
intro.content: You are a mid-level Product Manager at Roamly, a growth-stage consumer app that helps friend groups plan trips together. Today you own a messy activation problem: people create group trips and invite friends, but too many groups stall before choosing dates, budget, and itinerary details.
intro.imageBrief: Original Roamly product office, first-person PM laptop on a desk in a bright growth-stage consumer-app workspace. The single laptop shows a warm cream product dashboard with short neutral labels for group trips, invite conversion, budget preferences, and itinerary completion. Beside it are simple sticky notes with short product-topic words only. In the background, ordinary adult design and engineering coworkers, age 25-45, gather near a non-digital whiteboard with a simple trip-planning flow. Use a fresh room layout and fresh people; do not copy the reference image composition, people, furniture, window, table, wheelchair, prosthetic limb, tablet, or monitor. Exactly one digital device: the foreground laptop. No external monitor, desktop display, second laptop, tablet, iPad, phone, TV, wall display, screen-like dark rectangle, wheelchair, mobility aid, prosthetic limb, elderly person, sample answer text, or typed response.

briefing_kickoff.title: Monday Product Source Workspace
briefing_kickoff.content: Your job today: use Maya's ask and the funnel headline to audit the current Roamly flow. User evidence and constraints will carry forward as you synthesize the problem and draft the PRD slice.
briefing_kickoff.imageBrief: First-person PM laptop on a desk showing a cream Roamly product-review workspace. The screen contains only source evidence: a simple funnel chart with the provided percentages and brief user-quote cards. A small Slack notification from Maya is fully inside the laptop screen, not floating outside the screen edge. Printed notes on the desk are neutral source snippets only, not recommendations or answers. Original room and ordinary adult coworkers only. Laptop only; no monitor, tablet, phone, wheelchair, mobility aid, prosthetic limb, elderly person, sample answer text, or typed response.

messages.body: Before standup, open the Roamly source workspace and come back with the problem shape. Use the funnel, quotes, constraints, and candidate tickets; do not jump straight to a feature bet.
messages.lookFor: Use this Slack message to understand the PM lead’s ask, decision deadline, and which evidence should guide the opportunity choice.

apps.label: Analytics
apps.title: Trip planning funnel

rows.metric: Dates or budget completed by 3+ people

apps.lookFor: Use these funnel rows to see where group-trip planning stalls and which segment shows the clearest pain.

inputArtifacts.label: Slack and funnel dashboard
inputArtifacts.summary: Maya’s request plus the activation funnel; detailed Drive files move into later PM work surfaces.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Open the source apps and files before auditing the current Roamly flow.
continuity: Source facts reappear in later app tabs, work surfaces, and conversation prep.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The player starts from realistic workplace messages, apps, folders, and dashboards rather than a generic briefing bundle.

memoryCard.title: Project memory
bullets: Roamly groups often stall after trip creation and invites.
Maya wants a crisp problem read before feature brainstorming.
Detailed quotes, constraints, and bets come into the later PM tasks.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to invent extra context here. For now, hold onto the specific handoff I just gave you and keep moving.

turns.coworkerLine: Roamly helps friend groups plan trips, but the pattern is clear: people create a trip, invite friends, and then too many groups stall before the real decisions happen.
answerFacts: Roamly helps friend groups plan trips.
The activation problem is that groups stall after trip creation and invites.

turns.coworkerLine: This morning I need the problem shape, not a feature pile. Tell me where planning momentum is breaking before we talk about what to build.
answerFacts: Maya wants the problem shape first.
Feature bets should come after the team understands where planning momentum breaks.

turns.coworkerLine: The funnel is enough to orient you. For the problem notes, use analytics and your app-audit observations; pull in quotes, constraints, and tickets later when comparing bets.
answerFacts: The funnel is enough for orientation.
Problem-note synthesis should use analytics plus app-audit observations.
Quotes, constraints, and tickets belong later in bet comparison.

scene_02_app_audit.title: Audit the Current Roamly Flow
scene_02_app_audit.content: The funnel shows where group momentum drops. Now inspect the current app flow to form cause hypotheses before you refine the problem notes. Some Roamly buttons are marked with Try so you can see what the current product does next.
scene_02_app_audit.prompt: Write one short observation for each point. Focus on what the current experience makes easy, unclear, or socially awkward. Do not propose a finished solution yet.

companion.title: Ask Jordan
companion.fallbackPrompt: Voice unavailable? Type a quick question to Jordan.
companion.systemGuidance: Jordan is a peer Product Manager looking at the same Roamly screen. Jordan should help the player observe evidence and think like a PM, but must not write the audit note or reveal a final answer.

screens.title: Create Trip
screens.subtitle: A Roamly organizer creates a Montreal weekend trip and adds a few basics before inviting friends.

sections.label: Trip basics
sections.body: Dates, budget, saved places, votes, and itinerary all appear as setup areas.

sections.label: Organizer task
sections.body: The organizer can start everything, but the app does not yet show which shared decision matters first.

screens.footerNote: The first screen makes the trip feel started, but not yet coordinated.

screens.title: Invite Friends
screens.subtitle: Roamly shows who has joined and who is still pending.

listItems.label: Jo
listItems.detail: Joined; saved places only; no dates

screens.footerNote: Waiting for invited friends to join.

screens.title: Dates and Budget
screens.subtitle: Roamly collects date and budget inputs from each friend.

sections.label: Group responses
sections.body: Nina and Jo added dates. Ari has not added a budget. Sam has not responded.

sections.label: Budget request
sections.body: Roamly asks friends to add a budget range so the group can check them and plan.

actions.label: Preview budget request
actions.resultBody: Add your budget range when you can so the group can check them and plan.

screens.footerNote: The request focuses on getting more budget ranges into the trip.

screens.title: Saved Places
screens.subtitle: Friends vote for places they would be happy to include.

actions.label: View vote details
actions.resultBody: Each friend can select multiple places they would be happy to include.

resultItems.label: Nina
resultItems.detail: Selected Old Port walk, Bagel crawl, Rooftop dinner, Museum pass.

resultItems.label: Ari
resultItems.detail: Selected Old Port walk, Bagel crawl, Rooftop dinner.

resultItems.label: Jo
resultItems.detail: Selected Old Port walk, Bagel crawl, Rooftop dinner, Jazz bar.

screens.footerNote: Vote totals update as friends select places.

screens.title: AI Itinerary
screens.subtitle: Roamly can either leave the organizer with a blank plan or generate a full AI itinerary from the trip information it already has.
screens.emptyStateBody: Manual planning starts from a blank full-day draft. Roamly AI can also generate a share-ready itinerary in one tap.
screens.textBoxPlaceholder: Manual option: write the full day plan here...

actions.label: Generate AI itinerary
actions.resultBody: Saturday: 9:00 Old Port walk -> 11:00 Bagel crawl -> 2:00 Museum pass -> 6:30 Rooftop dinner -> 9:00 Jazz bar. Roamly AI made a share-ready plan from saved places, votes, dates, and budget responses without marking what is confirmed or still needs a group decision.

observations.label: Invite follow-through
observations.metricLink: Related metric: At least 1 friend joins
observations.prompt: What does this screen help the organizer understand, and what does it leave unresolved?
observations.placeholder: Example: The status list shows activity, but...
companionNudges: Compare what the status list shows with what the organizer can do from this screen.
Keep the observation grounded in the visible invite status and available actions.
Avoid proposing a solution before naming the unresolved part of the flow.

observations.label: Budget and date pressure
observations.metricLink: Related metric: dates or budget completed by 3+ people
observations.prompt: What might make this step easy for friends to avoid or misunderstand?
observations.placeholder: Example: The budget request may feel...
companionNudges: Read the request copy as a friend receiving it.
Notice what the app asks friends to share and what that wording might imply.
Keep the note about the date/budget step, not a full feature proposal.

observations.label: Saved-place priority ambiguity
observations.prompt: After viewing the vote details, why is it still hard for the organizer to choose what should go into the plan first?
observations.placeholder: Example: The place list gives the group options, but...
companionNudges: Look at the top vote counts and then open the vote details.
Notice whether votes show a first choice or just acceptable options.
Keep the note about what the organizer can infer from voting.

observations.label: AI full-plan jump
observations.prompt: What does the AI itinerary make look decided, and what does it fail to separate from unresolved group choices?
observations.placeholder: Example: The AI draft looks share-ready, but...
companionNudges: Press Generate AI itinerary and read what the draft assumes.
Compare the AI draft with incomplete votes, dates, budget, and response data.
Notice whether the plan separates confirmed choices from items the group still needs to decide.

inputArtifacts.label: Current Roamly app screens
inputArtifacts.summary: Invite, date/budget, saved places, and itinerary states in the current product.

inputArtifacts.label: Funnel context
inputArtifacts.summary: Activation metrics that show where group momentum drops.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Inspect current app screens, try marked product actions, ask Jordan as needed, and write cause-hypothesis observations.
continuity: The audit notes appear as a tab in the problem-note workspace and inform user-call prep.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: PMs often touch the current product after seeing funnel data so they can turn drop-off metrics into testable cause hypotheses.

The note fields are blank, Try buttons are visible but not opened, and no answer text is filled in. Laptop only; no external monitor, tablet, phone outside the app preview, wheelchair, mobility aid, prosthetic limb, elderly person, sample answer text, or typed response.

scene_02_problem_brief.title: Refine the App Audit Notes
scene_02_problem_brief.content: Use your current-flow audit to turn each observation into a clearer PM problem note. Do not choose a solution yet. You are preparing hypotheses to test in the user call.

rows.metric: Dates or budget completed by 3+ people

sourceTabs.label: Current App Audit
sourceTabs.content: Your notes from the current Roamly flow. Use these as cause hypotheses, not as final proof.
sourceTabs.sourceBindingEmptyText: No app audit notes saved yet.

scene_02_problem_brief.prompt: Refine each app-audit observation into a PM problem note for the user call.

fields.label: Observed friction
fields.placeholder: What does the current app screen make visible?

fields.label: Why it may matter
fields.placeholder: How could this friction affect friend participation, dates/budget completion, or itinerary completion?

fields.label: Cause hypothesis
fields.placeholder: Why might this friction be happening? Keep it as a hypothesis, not proof.

fields.label: Question for Nina
fields.placeholder: What should you ask Nina to confirm, refine, or challenge this note?

inputArtifacts.label: Analytics
inputArtifacts.summary: Activation funnel and 7-day itinerary completion goal.

inputArtifacts.label: Current app audit notes
inputArtifacts.summary: Student observations from the current Roamly flow.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Refine each current-app observation into a PM problem note with impact, cause hypothesis, and user-call validation question.
continuity: The problem notes inform research prep and PRD writing.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: PMs often refine early observations into testable notes before deciding which problem deserves a solution bet.

transition_research.title: Stand-up Ends with One Open Question
transition_research.content: Stand-up is reviewing the problem notes before the team chooses what to learn next.

aiResponseCheck.targetMeaning: The notes should preserve the four app-audit frictions as separate hypotheses: invite follow-through, date/budget pressure, saved-place priority ambiguity, and the AI full-plan jump that makes unresolved choices look decided. They should connect each note to a metric or behavior and identify what to validate with Nina.
aiResponseCheck.matchedContent: The team builds on your problem notes: you kept the current-app frictions separate instead of forcing one answer too early. Maya asks you to use the notes as hypotheses and validate them with a frequent organizer before narrowing the Q3 bet.
aiResponseCheck.fallbackContent: In stand-up, Maya treats the app-audit notes as early hypotheses, not a final problem statement. She asks you to validate which friction actually matters most with a frequent organizer before the team narrows the Q3 bet.
aiResponseCheck.loadingContent: Stand-up is reviewing your problem notes before the team chooses what to learn next.

transition_research.imageBrief: Original product office transition after stand-up. A small product trio of ordinary adult coworkers leaves a meeting room; a laptop on the table shows a calendar tile for a user interview and a neutral Group Momentum diagram. Whiteboard text is minimal and not an answer. Fresh room and people; do not copy the reference image. Laptop only; no monitor, tablet, phone, wheelchair, mobility aid, prosthetic limb, elderly person, sample answer text, or typed response.

scene_03_research_prep.title: Prepare the User Call
scene_03_research_prep.content: Nina Brooks organizes two or three friend trips a year and recently used Roamly for a weekend in Montreal. Prepare questions that test your app-audit hypotheses without leading her toward your favorite feature.
scene_03_research_prep.prompt: Write 5 user interview questions plus one sentence explaining what you are trying to learn.

appTabs.label: User Profile
appTabs.content:
Nina Brooks
- 29, lives in Chicago
- Usually plans trips with 5-8 friends
- Used Roamly for a Montreal weekend trip
- Created a trip and invited friends
- The group did not finish planning inside Roamly

appTabs.label: Research Guardrails
appTabs.content:
Avoid leading questions like: Would smart reminders fix this?
Ask exactly 5 prepared questions about actual behavior, recent examples, emotions, workarounds, decision points, and moments when the group lost momentum.
Use your app audit notes as hypotheses to test, not as facts to confirm.
During the call, ask one question, listen to Nina response, then ask at most one brief follow-up if her answer needs clarification.

appTabs.label: Your Problem Notes
appTabs.content: Use the problem notes and current app audit you just wrote as background. During the call, listen for evidence that confirms, refines, or challenges your hypotheses.

inputArtifacts.label: Nina user profile
inputArtifacts.summary: Trip organizer profile and recent Roamly behavior.

inputArtifacts.label: Research guardrails
inputArtifacts.summary: Guidance to avoid leading questions and learn from actual behavior.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Draft non-leading user interview questions and a learning goal.
continuity: This prep note appears in the voice meeting through prepNoteKey.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: PMs often prepare research questions from a user profile and prior product evidence before a customer conversation.

scene_04_user_call.title: Remote User Interview with Nina
scene_04_user_call.content: Use your prep questions, but listen actively. Your goal is to understand what happens when a real friend group loses planning momentum.
scene_04_user_call.meetingContext: A 20-minute remote user interview with Nina Brooks, a frequent Roamly organizer who planned a Montreal weekend trip with seven friends.
scene_04_user_call.prepReferenceContent: Nina organized a Montreal weekend trip with a friend group and used Roamly before the final plan moved outside the app. Your goal is to learn what happened, where the group stalled, and what kind of help would have felt natural.
scene_04_user_call.goalPrompt: You are Nina Brooks, a friendly but busy Roamly user who organizes friend-group trips. The player is a Product Manager interviewing you. Answer like a real user, not a product expert. Treat each player turn as one interview question or one brief follow-up: answer that question in one response, then pause for the next question. Do not ask your own interview questions. Do not volunteer every issue at once. If the player asks about saved places or voting, explain that the Montreal group saved 18 places and only 3 of 7 friends voted. If the player asks about dates or budget, explain that budget felt awkward and made people quiet. If the player asks about reminders or nudges, say reminders can help only if they do not make the organizer sound bossy. If the player asks how planning finished or what would have helped, explain that final decisions happened in group text and that you needed help getting everyone to make one small decision, not a whole planning system. If the player asks a broad opening question, share one concrete recent moment and wait for the next question. If the player asks leading questions, gently answer from your real behavior instead of endorsing their proposed feature. After the player covers actual recent behavior, group decision friction, and what would have helped at the moment of stall, wrap up with: "That is probably the main thing: I need help getting everyone to make one small decision, not a whole planning system." Do not introduce new topics after that closing signal.

requiredDisclosure.intro: Oh, one thing I should make sure I say:

items.text: For the Montreal trip, we saved 18 places.
Only 3 of the 7 friends actually voted.
What I needed was help getting everyone to make one small decision, not a whole planning system.

initialMessages.content: Hi, I have about 20 minutes. Happy to talk about the Montreal trip; Roamly helped us collect ideas, but the planning still got messy.

inputArtifacts.label: Student's user-call prep
inputArtifacts.summary: Questions the student prepared in the prior scene.

inputArtifacts.label: Compact user context
inputArtifacts.summary: Thin user context that leaves key friction details for the interview.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Conduct a spoken user interview that explores behavior and motivation without leading the user.
continuity: Interview insights inform the PRD slice.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: PMs frequently use customer or user conversations to refine product hypotheses before prioritization.

scene_04_user_call.imageBrief: Remote user interview setup on the PM laptop. The cream meeting window shows Nina Brooks, an ordinary adult Roamly user, on video in her apartment with a suitcase and small Montreal postcard. A compact side panel shows neutral source labels and blank prep-note lines, not the student's prepared questions. The meeting UI stays fully inside the laptop screen. The PM is visible only as hands near the keyboard and notebook. Laptop only; no monitor, tablet, phone, wheelchair, mobility aid, prosthetic limb, elderly person, sample answer text, or typed response.
scene_04_user_call.playerGoal: Ask your 5 prepared questions about Nina recent group trip planning stall. Use your app-audit hypotheses, but do not pitch your solution. After each answer, ask at most one brief follow-up only if it helps clarify behavior, motivation, workarounds, or what would have helped.
scene_04_user_call.endpoint: End once you understand the recent behavior, the group decision friction, and whether the current app experience failed to help the group make one small next decision.
scene_04_user_call.successCriteria: You ask each question clearly, let Nina answer without confusion, and use optional follow-ups only when they clarify a useful detail without steering her.

scene_07_prd_slice.title: Draft the PRD Slice
scene_07_prd_slice.content: Write the decision memo / PRD slice Maya can share with design and engineering. It should be specific enough to align the team, but scoped enough to avoid pretending everything is solved.
scene_07_prd_slice.prompt: Draft a concise PRD slice for your recommended next bet.
scene_07_prd_slice.placeholder:
Problem
...
Target user
...
Hypothesis
...
Success metric
...
Scope / non-goals
...
Risks and open questions
...

appTabs.label: Problem Notes
appTabs.content:
Problem notes to carry forward
Friend groups are creating trips and inviting people, but momentum drops before shared date, budget, and itinerary decisions.

Evidence to use
- Dates or budget completed by 3+ people: 19%.
- First-day itinerary completed: 11%.
- Current app audit notes separated unresolved invite status, avoidable budget sharing, votes that lack priority, and AI full-plan generation that makes unresolved choices look decided.
- Nina interview evidence should confirm, refine, or challenge which note deserves the next product bet.

Risk/unknown
Reminder-style features could feel pushy, budget sharing may be sensitive, and notification changes touch a fragile service.

appTabs.label: Decision Notes
appTabs.content:
Use your problem notes and Nina interview evidence to name one next bet. Strong candidates should help a group make one small decision, preserve user control, and create learning without treating the existing one-tap AI itinerary as a solved planning system.

Tempting ideas to scrutinize
- Expanding the AI full-itinerary generator: visible in the current flow, but broad and weak for the immediate stall because it does not separate confirmed choices from unresolved group decisions.
- Booking integration: partner dependency and not directly tied to the invite-to-decision drop-off.
- Smart nudges: potentially useful, but tone, control, and notification-service risk need explicit scope.

appTabs.label: PRD Checklist
appTabs.content:
Include
- Problem and target user.
- Hypothesis connected to the source evidence.
- Success metric tied to group momentum, such as dates/budget completed by 3+ people or first-day itinerary completion within 7 days.
- Proposed scope and non-goals.
- Rollout or experiment plan.
- Risks/open questions.
- Design, engineering, data, or support dependencies.

inputArtifacts.label: Problem notes reminder
inputArtifacts.summary: Reminder to use the earlier problem notes and Nina evidence.

inputArtifacts.label: Decision notes
inputArtifacts.summary: Reminder to choose a scoped next bet from the problem notes, Nina interview evidence, and constraints.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Draft a concise PRD slice / decision memo.
continuity: This PRD slice is the final PM artifact before assessment.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Mid-level PMs are expected to turn discovery evidence and tradeoff reasoning into a written artifact the team can align around.

assessment_gate.title: The Product Planning Day Is Complete
assessment_gate.content: You have diagnosed the group momentum problem, interviewed a user, and drafted a PRD slice. Maya has enough to assess your PM judgment for this scenario.
assessment_gate.imageBrief: End-of-day original product office scene with a PM laptop on the desk. The laptop shows small warm cream thumbnail windows for the problem notes, user interview notes, and PRD slice, but no readable answers or typed responses. Fresh room and ordinary adult coworkers only if people appear. Laptop only; no monitor, tablet, phone, wheelchair, mobility aid, prosthetic limb, elderly person, sample answer text, or typed response.

grading.title: Assessment in Progress
grading.content: Your PM artifacts and conversation transcript are being assessed against the rubric.

final_report.title: Product Manager Simulation Report
final_report.content: Review your strengths and next steps as a product manager.

jordan.name: Jordan Lee
jordan.persona: Jordan is a peer Product Manager working beside the student on the same Roamly activation problem. Jordan is curious, practical, and collaborative, and speaks like a teammate thinking aloud rather than a manager, tutor, or answer key. Jordan helps the student notice evidence on the current screen, compare what the product knows with what it asks users to do, and turn confusion into a useful PM observation, but Jordan never writes the final audit note for the student.

nina.name: Nina Brooks
nina.persona: Nina is a busy but generous Roamly user who often organizes friend trips. She is not a product expert and speaks in concrete stories rather than frameworks. She liked saving places in Roamly, but her group still drifted into text messages when budget, dates, and final choices got awkward. She is open to helpful prompts, but she dislikes anything that makes her sound bossy to friends.

intro.welcomeBody: Roamly is a fictional growth-stage consumer app that helps friend groups plan trips by collecting saved places, coordinating preferences, and building shared itineraries. You are a mid-level Product Manager responsible for improving group momentum after a trip is created.

steps.label: The Company
steps.content: Roamly serves friend groups planning weekend trips, reunions, and shared vacations. Users invite friends, save places, vote on ideas, share budget/date preferences, and assemble an itinerary together.

steps.label: Your Role
steps.content: You own a product area, not the entire company. Your job is to diagnose the activation problem, learn from users, and turn evidence into a scoped PRD slice for design and engineering.

steps.label: How You Will Work
steps.content: You will read product artifacts, write PM deliverables, speak with a user, and draft a scoped PRD slice.

glossary.activation: The moment when a new or returning user reaches meaningful value in a product. For Roamly, activation means a group trip moves from creation to real collaborative planning.
glossary.funnel: A sequence of user steps measured as conversion rates, such as creating a trip, inviting friends, and completing an itinerary.
glossary.hypothesis: A testable explanation or bet about what user problem exists and what product change might improve it.
glossary.PRD: Product Requirements Document. A written artifact that explains the problem, goal, scope, success metrics, and constraints for a product initiative.
glossary.roadmap: A prioritized plan of product work over time. It is not just a list of ideas; it reflects strategy, tradeoffs, timing, and constraints.
glossary.scope: The boundaries of what a team is committing to build or test. Clear scope also says what is not included.
glossary.non-goal: Something the team explicitly chooses not to solve in the current effort, often to preserve focus.
glossary.tradeoff: A decision that accepts one cost or limitation to gain something more important, such as faster learning or lower engineering risk.
glossary.prototype: A lightweight version of an idea used to learn before building the full product experience.
glossary.success metric: The measurable outcome used to judge whether a product change worked.

scene_02_problem_brief.problem: Friend groups are creating trips and inviting people, but they lose momentum before making shared date, budget, and itinerary decisions.
scene_02_problem_brief.evidence: The funnel drops from 64% invited to 19% dates/budget completed by 3+ people and 11% first-day itinerary completed. The current app audit shows unclear next actions after invite, sensitive budget/date sharing, uneven voting on many saved places, and an AI itinerary draft that makes unresolved choices look decided.
scene_02_problem_brief.hypothesis: The organizer lacks a low-pressure way to move friends toward one small next decision, so planning drifts back to text.
scene_02_problem_brief.risk: Reminder-style features could feel pushy, budget sharing may be sensitive, and notification work has engineering risk.

freeTextResponses.appAuditNotes: {"invite_followthrough":"The invite screen shows who joined, but it does not tell the organizer what shared decision to ask for next. The group can look active while still making no planning progress.","budget_pressure":"The budget request asks friends to share a sensitive range without explaining why it matters or giving a low-pressure option. Friends may avoid it, which connects to the 19% dates/budget completion drop.","decision_overload":"The saved places list shows acceptable options, not a clear first choice. Three places have similar vote totals, so the organizer still has to infer what decision would unlock the plan.","next_decision":"The AI itinerary creates a share-ready plan even though dates, budget, and some votes are unresolved. It makes undecided choices look settled, which could create false confidence or social friction."}
freeTextResponses.problemBrief: [{"observedFriction":"The app shows join/pending status but not the next group decision.","whyItMayMatter":"Invites can happen without the group moving toward dates, budget, or itinerary completion.","causeHypothesis":"The organizer may not know which small decision would create momentum.","questionForNina":"After friends joined, what was the next decision you tried to get from the group?"},{"observedFriction":"Budget sharing feels personal and the request copy is generic.","whyItMayMatter":"Friends may delay or avoid responding, blocking group momentum.","causeHypothesis":"People need context, control, or a softer way to share constraints.","questionForNina":"How did your friends react when budget or dates came up?"},{"observedFriction":"Votes show acceptable places but not priority or tradeoffs.","whyItMayMatter":"The organizer still cannot confidently choose the first-day plan.","causeHypothesis":"Multiple okay votes do not reveal what the group wants most.","questionForNina":"When several places had support, how did you decide what to put first?"},{"observedFriction":"The AI draft looks final even when inputs are incomplete.","whyItMayMatter":"It may hide unresolved choices instead of helping the group decide.","causeHypothesis":"A full itinerary is too large a jump before one small agreement.","questionForNina":"Did a complete draft help your group decide, or did it create more debate?"}]
freeTextResponses.scene_03_research_prep:
Questions:
1. Walk me through the last moment when the Montreal planning slowed down.
2. What were you trying to get friends to decide at that point?
3. How did people respond when dates or budget came up?
4. What did you do outside Roamly to move the plan forward?
5. When Roamly showed a full itinerary draft, what felt decided and what still felt unresolved?

Learning goal: I want to learn which specific group decision caused momentum to stall, and whether Roamly should help the organizer move friends toward one small next decision rather than producing a full plan too early.
freeTextResponses.scene_07_prd_slice:
Problem: Friend groups start trips in Roamly, but momentum drops before enough people complete dates/budget or commit to a first-day itinerary. The funnel shows dates/budget completed by 3+ people at 19% and first-day itinerary completion at 11%.

Target user: Trip organizers planning with 5-8 friends who need to move the group from interest to one concrete shared decision.

Hypothesis: If Roamly helps the organizer ask for one low-pressure next decision, the group will make progress without feeling pushed into a full itinerary too early.

Recommended next bet: Create a Next small decision module after invites. It should identify one focused decision, such as date window, budget comfort, or first anchor activity. The organizer can send a lightweight prompt, see who responded, and keep unresolved choices visible.

Success metric: Increase trips where 3+ people complete date/budget inputs, and increase first-day itinerary completion within 7 days.

Scope: One decision prompt, response status, and organizer summary. Non-goals: no booking integration, no full AI itinerary expansion, no aggressive notification sequence.

Risks/open questions: Tone may feel pushy. Budget remains sensitive. Notification-service work should be limited. We need to test whether users prefer date, budget, or anchor-activity prompts first.

scene_04_user_call.content: Walk me through the last moment when the Montreal planning slowed down.
After people joined, nobody wanted to be the first to commit to dates or budget. We kept moving back to the group chat.
What were you trying to get friends to decide at that point?
I wanted one small decision: whether Saturday worked and what kind of budget people were comfortable with.
How did people respond when dates or budget came up?
Dates were fine, but budget felt awkward. People avoided it unless I gave them a reason.
What did you do outside Roamly to move the plan forward?
I sent a text asking people to pick between two date windows and say if rooftop dinner was too expensive.
When Roamly showed a full itinerary draft, what felt decided and what still felt unresolved?
It looked polished, but it assumed budget and priorities we had not agreed on. It made me nervous to share.

criteria.name: 現行画面調査
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 現行フローを実際に見て、摩擦、迷い、情報不足、測るべき行動を観察として整理できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 問題要約
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Analyticsタブのファネル指標とCurrent App Auditの観察だけを根拠に、各問題ノートで観察された摩擦、なぜ重要か、原因仮説、Ninaに確認する未解明点を分けて書けている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 利用者面談
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 準備メモではなく実際の面談で、利用者の行動、動機、困りごと、回避策を聞き取り、誘導を避けられている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 要件文書
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 要件文書だけを根拠に、問題、対象、成功条件、範囲外、未決事項、次の検証を明確に書けている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。
